coredata.dc:title: CdTe quantum dot-based fluorescent probes for selective detection of Hg (II): The effect of particle size
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jian
Zhao, Zhu-Jun
Li, Jian-Jun
Zhao, Jun-Wu
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ions-induced fluorescence quenching properties of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) have been studied using the fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. By using the hydrothermal method, the CdTe QDs with different particles sizes from 1.98 to 3.68nm have been prepared, and the corresponding fluorescence emission wavelength is changed from 518 to 620nm. The fluorescence of QDs is enhanced after linking Bovine serum albumin (BSA) onto the surface of the QDs. Experimental results show that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-coated CdTe QDs could be effectively quenched when Hg2+ react with BSA-coated CdTe QDs. Interestingly, both the sensing sensitivity and selectivity of this fluorescence probe could be improved when the particle size of the QDs decreases. Thus the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with green fluorescence emission have better advantages than the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with red fluorescence for Hg2+ detection. Interference experiment results indicate that the influence from other metal ions could be neglected in the detection, and the Hg2+ could be specifically detected. By using this BSA-coated CdTe QDs-based fluorescence probe, the Hg2+ could be detected with an ultra-low detection limit of nanomole level, and the linear range spans a scope from 0.001 to 1μmol/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdTe quantum dots (QDs)
Fluorescence
Quenching
Mercury ions
Particle size
Spectrum sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Preparation of CdTe QDs with Different Particle Sizes
Surface Modification and the Preparation of Fluorescent Probe
Optical Properties of CdTe Quantum Dots with Different Particle Sizes
The Effect of Hg2+ on the Fluorescence Quenching of CdTe Quantum Dot-based Fluorescent Probe
Improve the Sensing Ability for Hg2+ by Tuning the Particle Size of CdTe Quantum Dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of CdTe quantum dots with different heating reflux time.
(a) HRTEM image and (b) the size distribution of CdTe quantum dots with a fluorescence emission at 533nm (the corresponding size is 2.34nm and the heating reflux time is 20min).
(a) HRTEM image and (b) the size distribution of CdTe quantum dots with a fluorescence emission at 620nm (the corresponding size is 3.68nm and the heating reflux time is 48h).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CdTe quantum dots with different heating reflux time, (b) The fluorescence peak wavelength as a function of heating reflux time, the inset is the digital photograph of CdTe QDs under the ultraviolet light illumination.
The comparison of fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs before and after BSA coating.
(a) The effect of Hg2+ with different concentrations on the fluorescence quenching of CdTe quantum dot, (b) The dependence of ln(F0/F) on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions, d=3.68nm.

The fluorescence spectra of CdTe quantum dot in the presence of different metal ions. The concentration of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ is 50μmol/L, the concentration of Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ is 5μmol/L, The concentration of Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+ is 1μmol/L. d=2.34nm.

(a) The dependence of ln(F0/F) of CdTe quantum dot with different particle size on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions, the inset is the digital photograph of CdTe QDs with different particle size under the ultraviolet light illumination, (b) The fluorescence peak intensity of CdTe quantum dot with different particle size in the presence of different metal ions. d=2.34, 3.29 and 3.68nm.
Performance comparison of different fluorescence spectroscopy sensing methods.
Determination results of mercury in sample.

head.ce:title: CdTe quantum dot-based fluorescent probes for selective detection of Hg (II): The effect of particle size

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ions-induced fluorescence quenching properties of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) have been studied using the fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. By using the hydrothermal method, the CdTe QDs with different particles sizes from 1.98 to 3.68nm have been prepared, and the corresponding fluorescence emission wavelength is changed from 518 to 620nm. The fluorescence of QDs is enhanced after linking Bovine serum albumin (BSA) onto the surface of the QDs. Experimental results show that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-coated CdTe QDs could be effectively quenched when Hg2+ react with BSA-coated CdTe QDs. Interestingly, both the sensing sensitivity and selectivity of this fluorescence probe could be improved when the particle size of the QDs decreases. Thus the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with green fluorescence emission have better advantages than the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with red fluorescence for Hg2+ detection. Interference experiment results indicate that the influence from other metal ions could be neglected in the detection, and the Hg2+ could be specifically detected. By using this BSA-coated CdTe QDs-based fluorescence probe, the Hg2+ could be detected with an ultra-low detection limit of nanomole level, and the linear range spans a scope from 0.001 to 1μmol/L.

Fluorescence of CdTe QDs could be effectively quenched when Hg2+ has been adsorbed on the QDs. And the sensing sensitivity and selectivity could be improved when the particle size of the QDs is decreased.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As we know, heavy metals cause an immense danger to food safety and the environment. As a kind of toxic metallic ions, high level of Hg2+ in food and drinking water is detrimental to human health. Therefore, many analysis methods, such as electrochemical sensing [1], photoelectrochemical sensing [2], dye molecule–based or plasmonic colorimetric sensing [3,4], fluorescent sensor [5] and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) based fiber optic sensor [6], have been developed for the highly efficient, ultrasensitive and selective detection of Hg2+.

Because of its simplicity and high sensitivity, quantum dots-based fluorescence probe for detection of chemical and biologic molecules have become a hot topic in nanoscience and analysis [7–11]. Recently, the fluorescence properties of QDs have also been used in the detection of Hg2+. In the review of Vázquez-González et al., a detailed evaluation of the different approaches developed so far for the analysis of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ by using various types of QDs has been provided [12]. Wang et al. report the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots with a high quantum yield of 38.4% [13]. These nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots could be used for mercury ion sensing with a detection limit of 80nmol/L. By using the Cys-capped ZnS QDs, a highly sensitive and selective photoelectrochemical sensing method for Hg2+ detection was developed by Wang et al. [14]. The reported sensing method could be used for the detection of trace Hg2+ with a linear range of 0.01 to 10.0μmol/L and a detection limit of 4.6nmol/L. In the report of Liu et al., a green strategy for the production of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine nanoparticles has been investigated by UV light irradiation [15]. The fluorescent nanoparticles can serve as a fluorescent sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+. Jaiswal et al. reported the use of biopolymer-stabilized ZnS QDs for cation exchange reaction-based sensing of heavy metal ions including Hg2+, Ag+, and Pb2+ in water [16]. In the study of He et al., photo luminescent carbon dots have been synthesized using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine as the surface passivation reagent [17]. This fluorescent probe could be used for selective detection of Hg(II) with a linear range of 0–80μmol/L and a detection limit of 0.201mmol/L

As a kind of important semiconductor nanocrystals, CdTe QDs exhibit unique photophysical properties due to their high quantum yield, excellent photo stability, narrow emission wavelengths and size-dependent fluorescence frequency. Idowu et al. reported the luminescent water-soluble CdTe QDs capped with different thiol carboxylic acids [18]. Fluorescence enhancement of CdTe QDs was observed when in the mixture with BSA. The MPA-capped CdTe QDs were used to detect the concentration of BSA and the size of them is 2.3nm. In the report of Adegoke and Nyokong, MPA-capped CdTe QDs and CdTe@ZnS QDs have been employed as luminescent probes for the sensitive and selective sensing of peroxynitrite [19]. The size of MPA-CdTe QDs is 2.7nm, and the size of GSH-TGA-CdTe@ZnS QDs is 3.0nm. The limit of detection of 72.4nmol/L was obtained. Li et al. developed a hydrothermal synthesis method of CdTe QDs with higher photoluminescence intensity [20]. The sizes of TGA-capped CdTe QDs are 2.0±0.3nm and 6.0±0.9nm. These CdTe QDs could be labeled with bovine serum albumin (BSA) for fluorescence probes without pretreatment.

CdTe QDs have also been used for Hg2+ detection. Xia and Zhu found that the denatured bovine serum albumin-coated CdTe QDs could be quenched effectively by Hg2+, and the limit of detection for Hg2+ was 4.0×10−9 mol/L [21]. A highly selective and simple fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection based on cysteamine-capped CdTe QDs with size of 2.89nm has been reported by Ding et al. [22]. They found the fluorescence quenching effect of cysteamine-capped CdTe QDs was linear with Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 6.0–450nmol/L. Saikia et al. reported a simple and ultrasensitive sensing technique based on CdTe/ZnS QDs with the size of 5–6nm for the detection of toxic Hg(II) [23]. Because of the interaction between QDs and Hg(II), the photo luminescence of QDs has been quenched via excited state electron transfer mechanism. The application of water-soluble N-acetyl-l-cysteine-capped CdTe QDs for Hg(II) detection has also been studied [24]. It has been found the presence of Hg(II) ions could quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs with the size of 3nm at 565nm and produce new peak in 700–860nm. Liu et al. reported the surface plasmon-coupled emission of CdTe QDs and developed a fluorescent sensor for Hg(II) ion sensing [25]. They found that this surface plasmon-coupled emission-based sensing enlarged the response range and has higher sensitivity.

Although many efforts have been developed to improve the sensing ability of the CdTe QDs-based fluorescence probe, the effect of particle size on the detection limit and selectivity for Hg2+ has seldom been reported. In this paper, BSA-coated CdTe QDs with different particle sizes have been prepared and been used in the Hg2+ detection. It has been found the fluorescence of BSA-coated CdTe QDs could be quenched greatly by Hg2+. Furthermore, both the sensing sensitivity and selectivity of this fluorescence probe could be improved when the particle size of the QDs decreases.

In this study, the following reagents were necessary, and all the solutions were prepared with double deionized water (DDW). Tellurium powder (~100mesh, 99.99%), Cadmium chloride hemi (pentahydrate) (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 99.95%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, 96%), Mercuric chloride (HgCl2, >99%) were purchased from Aladdin, China. Thioglycolic Acid (TGA, 98%) was acquired from ACROS ORGANIC Inc., Belgium. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich, America. Glycine (AR, >99%) was purchased from Shanghai ShanPu Chemical Company Ltd., China. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, >99%) was obtained from Bioreagent Inc., America.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Preparation of CdTe QDs with Different Particle Sizes
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal thioglycolic-acid (TGA)-capped CdTe QDs were synthesized based on the method [21,26] with minor modification. Firstly, 200mL water to be used was boiled for 10min to remove oxygen inside. Then, 63.8mg Te powder and 60mg NaBH4 were added into a 50mL three-necked flask. After bubbling nitrogen through the flask for 30min, 4mL oxygen-free water was injected into the above flask. Then, the temperature was adjusted to 0°C by ice-bath and nitrogen gas was bubbled through the solution all the time. Meanwhile, the solution was stirred for 6h to synthesize NaHTe. Next, 228mg CdCl2·2.5H2O and 0.17mL (14.1mol/L) thioglycolic-acid were added into 100mL oxygen-free water in a 500mL three-necked flask. Last, adjusting the pH of the mixture to 10.5–11.0 with 1mol/L NaOH solution, and adding the freshly prepared NaHTe solution into the above mixture under stirring, the orange CdTe precursors were formed. To form the different sizes of QDs, the precursors were heated up at 100°C and refluxed for a certain period of time (5min, 20min, 40min, 90min, 3h, 6h, 8h, 10h, 24h, 36h, 48h). The QDs were precipitated with excess isopropyl alcohol whose volume is triple that of QDs. By using a 10mL centrifuge tube, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 5min to remove unreacted substance. Afterwards, the precipitates can be redissolved in DDW the same volume as previous amount and stored in fridge under 4°C for further use. The final concentration of prepared QDs was 1×10−2 mol/L, which was calculated according to the concentration of Cd2+. The fluorescence emission spectra of the QDs solutions were recorded with the condition of excitation wavelength at 400nm and the slit width of both excitation and emission at 5nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface Modification and the Preparation of Fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para: The surface modification is the process of linking BSA to CdTe QDs using the method reported by other group [20]. First, the as-prepared QDs with five sizes (2.34nm, 2.85nm, 3.29nm, 3.5nm and 3.68nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths are 533nm, 551nm, 570nm, 601nm and 620nm) were diluted to 1×10−3 mol/L. 100μL QDs reacted with 100μL NHS (4.3×10−5 mol/L) for 10min to activate the carboxylic end of the QDs. Then the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4 by adding 4.7mL phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 1×10−4 mol/L, pH=7.4) solution followed by injecting 100μL BSA (7.5×10−5 mol/L). Here, the concentration ratio of BSA and CdTe QDs is 0.075 (CBSA /CQDs =0.075) and fixed. Then the solution was stirred for 30min at 37°C. After adding 100μL Glycine (1.3×10−3 mol/L) without any purification into the reaction mixture as protective agent, the solution was shook for another 30min. The same process was performed without addition of BSA solution, which can be made as blank control trial. The final concentration of the BSA-coated CdTe QDs is 2×10−5 mol/L according to the concentration of CdTe QDs. All the samples were stored in fridge overnight below 4°C.

The method was learned from the literature [21]. 5mL BSA-coated QDs (CQDs =2×10−5 mol/L) with three different sizes (2.34nm, 3.29nm and 3.68nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths are 533nm, 570nm and 620nm) and 0.5mL PBS (1×10−4 mol/L, pH=7.4) buffer solution were added to the tubes. Different volume of 10μmol/L Hg2+ stock solution (0μL, 1μL, 10μL, 50μL, 100μL, 200μL, 400μL, 600μL, 800μL, 1mL) were added to the above solution sequentially. The solution was diluted to 10mL with ultrapure water and shook homogeneously for 5min. The final concentrations of Hg2+ of these solutions are 0μmol/L, 0.001μmol/L, 0.01μmol/L, 0.05μmol/L, 0.1μmol/L, 0.2μmol/L, 0.4μmol/L, 0.6μmol/L, 0.8μmol/L, 1μmol/L. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of all solutions were measured with the condition the same as that of the CdTe QDs.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images of CdTe QDs were taken with a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). Centrifugation was carried out by using a 5810R centrifuge (Eppendorf, Japan). Absorption spectra were measured using a UV-3600 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence spectra were collected using HORIBA JOBIN YVON fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, France) with the excitation of 400nm and silt width of 5nm. A Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA) was used to acquire the deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical Properties of CdTe Quantum Dots with Different Particle Sizes
ce:section.ce:para:
Absorption spectra of CdTe QDs with different heating reflux times are shown in Fig. 1 . One can find the CdTe QDs exhibit an absorption peak in the visible region. When the heating time is 5min, the absorption peak appears at about 485nm. As the heating time increases, the absorption peak red shifts obviously and fades down slightly. The red shift of the absorption peak indicates that the band gap energy decreases and the corresponding particle size of the QDs increases. By using the wavelength of the absorption peaks, the sizes of the synthesized CdTe QDs could be calculated according to the following equation in the reference [19].
d =
9.8127 ×
10
− 7
λ 3
−
1.7147 ×
10
− 3
λ 2
+
1.0064
λ − 194.84
3.1

In this formula, λ is the absorption maximum of the QDs, and d is the particle size of the QDs. When the heating time increases from 5min to 48h, we can obtain the QDs with the calculated size of 1.98nm, 2.34nm, 2.6nm, 2.85nm, 3.12nm, 3.24nm, 3.29nm, 3.38nm, 3.5nm, 3.6nm and 3.68nm. To obtain the HRTEM image of the prepared QDs, the original QDs with the fluorescence emissions at 533nm and 620nm were centrifuged 8000rpm for 5min separately after adding isopropyl alcohol whose volume is triple that of QDs. Then the precipitates were redissolved in DDW and concentrated to 0.1mol/L. The morphology of the QDs with the fluorescence emissions at 533nm and 620nm could be observed from the HRTEM images in Figs. 2a and 3a . The CdTe QDs have uniform size and distribution, and their shapes are nearly spherical, and the corresponding size distribution of the QDs with the fluorescence emission at 533nm and 620nm were shown in Figs. 2b and 3b. Among the plots, one can find the QDs own centralized sizes, and the average particle sizes of the QDs are found to be 2.35nm and 3.72nm respectively. The sizes are in close agreement with the calculated results from the above fitting equation.

Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence spectra of CdTe quantum dots with different heating reflux time. In the spectra, strong fluorescence emission peaks can be observed in the visible region. By varying the heating reflux time at several different temperatures, CdTe QDs with different fluorescence wavelengths were obtained. As the heating time increases from 5min to 48h, the fluorescence wavelength could be well tuned from 518 to 620nm. The red-shifts of the emission of CdTe QDs are attributed to the decrease of the band gap energy which induced by the increase of the particle size [20]. The relationship between fluorescence emission maximum and heating reflux time is described in Fig. 4b. As the heating time increases, the fluorescence peak red shifts nonlinearly. The speed of red shift reduces gradually when the heating time increases. The inset of Fig. 4b shows the colors of CdTe QDs with different sizes under the ultraviolet light illumination. One can observe the vivid color from the QDs, which changes from green to yellow and red as the heating time increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Effect of Hg2+ on the Fluorescence Quenching of CdTe Quantum Dot-based Fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to decrease the electron trap and improve the fluorescence quantum yield of the CdTe QDs, surface modification of QDs by coating BSA is carried out in this study. Here, CdTe QDs with five different particle sizes (2.34nm, 2. 85nm, 3.29nm, 3.5nm and 3.68nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths are 533nm, 551nm, 570nm, 601nm and 620nm) are used to conjunct with BSA. The difference between BSA-coated CdTe QDs and blank control samples is shown in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence spectra in this figure showed that the fluorescence intensity of the corresponding CdTe QDs has been enhanced distinctly when the CdTe QDs are coated with BSA molecule via coordination of the carboxylic group on the QDs with the amine group on BSA [18]. The increase of the fluorescence intensity indicates that the process of conjunction between CdTe QDs and BAS is successful [20].

The fluorescence spectra of BSA-modified CdTe QDs with the addition of Hg2+ with different concentrations are compared in Fig. 6a. In this case, the diameter of the QDs is 3.68nm and its corresponding fluorescence emission wavelength is 620nm. When 1μL Hg2+ (10μmol/L) are added in the 5mL BSA-coated QDs (2×10−5 mol/L) followed by diluting to 10mL, the fluorescence intensity decreases from 682,400 to 636,500 (CPS). Thus the detecting limit of Hg2+ could reach to 1nmol/L by measuring the intensity decrease of the fluorescence peak. The fluorescence quenching mechanism could be described from two aspects. First, Hg2+ bind onto the surface of QDs, which facilitates non-radiation recombination of holes in the valance bands and excitation electrons in the conduction bands [21]. Second, electrons effectively transfer from surface traps of BSA-coated CdTe QDs to Hg2+, which leads to the formation of the ground-state charge transfer complex. The complex can absorb exciting light and prevent the radiation combination between excitation electrons and holes, which results in fluorescence quenching of QDs [27]. As the concentration of Hg2+ of the solution increases gradually, the fluorescence intensity continues to decrease monotonously. When the Hg2+ concentration of the solution increases to 1μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity decreases to 14,420 (CPS), which is too weak to be observed distinctly. Based on the experimental result that Hg2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of BSA-coated CdTe QDs, the BSA-coated CdTe QDs could be developed as a fluorescence probe for the ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity (In(F0/F)) of CdTe QDs and the concentration of Hg2+ (C, 10−7 mol/L) is plotted in Fig. 6b, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the Hg2+. One can find a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9944) exhibited between the degree of fluorescence quenching and the concentration of Hg2+. The corresponding linear regression equation is shown in Fig. 6b. The linear relation spans a large concentration range from 0.001 to 1μmol/L, and the detection limit could reach 1nmol/L. This fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection based on the CdTe QDs is competitive with or even better than other fluorescence spectral sensing methods, as shown in Table 1 .

In this section, to evaluate the selectivity of this fluorescence probe in the detection of Hg2+, ten kinds of metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ have reacted with the prepared BSA-coated CdTe QDs. In this test, the diameter of the QDs is 2.34nm. The concentration of Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+ has a same value of 1μmol/L. The concentration of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ is 50μmol/L, which is fifty-fold of the concentration of Hg2+. The concentration of Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ is 5μmol/L, which is five-fold of the concentration of Hg2+. Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs in the presence of different metal ions. One can find the intense fluorescence emission of CdTe QDs has been completely quenched by Hg2+. No emission peak could be observed in the fluorescence spectrum of CdTe QDs with the addition of Hg2+. However, other metal ions can't quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs completely. One can't observe distinct intensity decrease of fluorescence emission when K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ or Pb2+ is added. Although the addition of Cu2+ and Ag+ results in distinct fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence peaks still take place in the spectra. Thus Hg2+ could be selectively detected by using this CdTe QDs fluorescence probe. Fig. S1 compared the fluorescence peak intensity of CdTe quantum dot in the presence of different metal ions. As shown in Fig. S1, these interfering ions except that of Ag+ and Cu2+ almost had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs, which guarantees the good selectivity of this sensing method. The excellent selectivity and specificity can be explained in detail as below. The fluorescence quenching induced by Hg2+ could be attributed to two reasons: First, Hg2+ ions bind onto the surface of QDs, and this process promotes the non-radiation recombination of holes in the valance bands and excitation electrons in the conduction bands [21]. Second, effective electrons transfer from BSA-coated QDs to Hg2+ leads to the decrease of fluorescence emission [27]. Furthermore, the latter plays an important role in the fluorescence quenching of QDs. However, most other ions like K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Pb2+ have no affinity toward the surface of QDs. The reason why Cu2+ and Ag+ can quench fluorescence of BSA-coated CdTe QDs at some degree is that Cu2+ and Ag+ bind onto the surface of QDs, which induce the non-radiation recombination of holes and electrons. While Cu2+, and Ag+ have certain affinity toward the QDs [32,33], their effect is too weak compared with that of Hg2+ because there are no electron transfer between the BSA-coated QDs and Cu2+ and Ag+. So the lowest fluorescence intensity and outstanding selectivity are obtained for the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with the addition of Hg2+.

In this section, to evaluate the selectivity of this fluorescence probe in the detection of Hg2+, ten kinds of metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ have reacted with the prepared BSA-coated CdTe QDs. In this test, the diameter of the QDs is 2.34nm. The concentration of Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+ has a same value of 1μmol/L. The concentration of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ is 50μmol/L, which is fifty-fold of the concentration of Hg2+. The concentration of Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ is 5μmol/L, which is five-fold of the concentration of Hg2+. Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs in the presence of different metal ions. One can find the intense fluorescence emission of CdTe QDs has been completely quenched by Hg2+. No emission peak could be observed in the fluorescence spectrum of CdTe QDs with the addition of Hg2+. However, other metal ions can't quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs completely. One can't observe distinct intensity decrease of fluorescence emission when K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ or Pb2+ is added. Although the addition of Cu2+ and Ag+ results in distinct fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence peaks still take place in the spectra. Thus Hg2+ could be selectively detected by using this CdTe QDs fluorescence probe. Fig. S1 compared the fluorescence peak intensity of CdTe quantum dot in the presence of different metal ions. As shown in Fig. S1, these interfering ions except that of Ag+ and Cu2+ almost had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs, which guarantees the good selectivity of this sensing method. The excellent selectivity and specificity can be explained in detail as below. The fluorescence quenching induced by Hg2+ could be attributed to two reasons: First, Hg2+ ions bind onto the surface of QDs, and this process promotes the non-radiation recombination of holes in the valance bands and excitation electrons in the conduction bands [21]. Second, effective electrons transfer from BSA-coated QDs to Hg2+ leads to the decrease of fluorescence emission [27]. Furthermore, the latter plays an important role in the fluorescence quenching of QDs. However, most other ions like K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Pb2+ have no affinity toward the surface of QDs. The reason why Cu2+ and Ag+ can quench fluorescence of BSA-coated CdTe QDs at some degree is that Cu2+ and Ag+ bind onto the surface of QDs, which induce the non-radiation recombination of holes and electrons. While Cu2+, and Ag+ have certain affinity toward the QDs [32,33], their effect is too weak compared with that of Hg2+ because there are no electron transfer between the BSA-coated QDs and Cu2+ and Ag+. So the lowest fluorescence intensity and outstanding selectivity are obtained for the BSA-coated CdTe QDs with the addition of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improve the Sensing Ability for Hg2+ by Tuning the Particle Size of CdTe Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the best performance for the detection of Hg2+, CdTe QDs fluorescence probes with three different particle sizes have been prepared and used in the Hg2+ detection. The dependence of ln(F0/F) of CdTe QDs with different particle sizes on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions is shown in Fig. 8a, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the Hg2+. As the particles size decreases from 3.68 to 2.34nm, the slope of the fitting line increases distinctly. Thus, the fluorescence emission of the BSA-coated QDs with smaller size is more sensitive to the concentrations of Hg2+ ions. The inset shows the digital photograph of BSA-coated CdTe QDs with different particle sizes under the ultraviolet light illumination. When the concentrations of Hg2+ ions of the solution increase from 0.001 to 1μmol/L, the fluorescence emission of BSA-coated QDs quenches gradually. The change of colors can be observed by naked eye. However, the fluorescence emission of BSA-coated QDs is more sensitive to the Hg2+ concentration when the QDs have a small particle size of 2.34nm. Thus the BSA-coated QDs with green fluorescence are easier to be quenched by Hg2+. Fig. 8b shows the fluorescence emission peak intensity of BSA-coated CdTe QDs with different particle sizes in the presence of different metal ions. One can find the capacity of resisting interference of the probe could be improved by decreasing the size of the QDs. And the CdTe QDs with smaller size also exhibit lower detection limit.

In this section, we discuss the physical origin of the effect from the aspect of particles size. The specific surface of the QDs increases rapidly as the size of the QDs decreases. Thus the QDs with smaller size have more surface electrons and high reactivity, which make it more convenient for them to coordinate with Hg2+ ions [22,34]. Therefore, the QDs with smaller size are more easily to be quenched by Hg2+ ions. Furthermore, the QDs with smaller size possess bigger specific surface than larger ones, and they can be coated more closely by the same concentration of BSA. It effectively prevents the other metal ions except for Hg2+ from binding on the surface of QDs, which reduces non-radiation recombination of holes and electrons. Thus, small-sized CdTe QDs exhibit higher detection sensitivity and lower detection limit.

To examine the practicality of the proposed method of determining the concentration of Hg2+, real samples test is carried out in this trial by detecting the Hg2+ in tap water. The results of recovery experience are shown in Table 2 . The table shows that the method has good repetition, high accuracy and was simple and rapid. The recovery rate of this method is found varying from 96.02% to 107.2%, and the relative standard deviation of it varies from 3.1% to 7.5%. The results indicate that the presented method for mercury ion detection is reliable and practical.

In this study, CdTe QDs with different particles size and fluorescence emission have been prepared using the hydrothermal method. Experimental results indicate the fluorescence intensity of the BSA-coated CdTe QDs could be effectively quenched by Hg2+. By using this QDs-based fluorescence probe, the Hg2+ could be detected with an ultra-low detection limit of 1nmol/L, and the linear range spans a large concentration range from 0.001 to 1μmol/L. By decreasing the particle size of the QDs, the sensing sensitivity and selectivity of this fluorescence probe could be further improved. Also, this fluorescence probe has the advantages of simple fabrication and convenient operation, which provides an effective strategy for design of BSA-coated QDs-based fluorescence sensor to detect heavy metals.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
The fluorescence peak intensity of CdTe quantum dot in the presence of different metal ions. The concentration of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ is 50μmol/L, the concentration of Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ is 5μmol/L, The concentration of Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+ is 1μmol/L. d=2.34nm.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.01.043.
